coredata.dc:title: Novel intelligent naked-eye food packaging pH-sensitive and fluorescent sulfur, nitrogen-carbon dots biosensors for tomato spoilage detection including DFT and molecular docking characterization

coredata.dc:description: Tomatoes are susceptible to microbial spoilage, leading to significant economic losses. This study introduces a novel approach to monitor tomato spoilage. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) films incorporated with sulfur and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (SN–CDs) were developed as pH-sensitive colorimetric sensors for tomatoes. The SN–CDs, derived from red onion peel waste (OPW), exhibited excellent fluorescence properties and antimicrobial activity XPS analysis confirmed the successful synthesis of SN–CDs with incorporated N and S. The incorporation of OPW significantly reduced the Young's modulus of the CMC hydrogel film, likely due to structural disruptions and increased free volume within the film. The color of the prepared colorimetric sensors was changed after tomato spoilage which means the effectiveness of these films in the tomato spoilage detection by naked eye. It exhibited pH-sensitivity because of the presence of flavonoids due to structural changes, including protonation and deprotonation. This pH sensitivity allows for visual indication of pH changes, as demonstrated by the CMC-SN-CDs3 film, which turns yellow in acidic conditions and red in alkaline conditions. The CMC-SN–CDs films displayed a distinct color change in response to pH variations, enabling visual detection of tomato spoilage. DFT calculations and molecular docking studies support the findings.

head.ce:title: Novel intelligent naked-eye food packaging pH-sensitive and fluorescent sulfur, nitrogen-carbon dots biosensors for tomato spoilage detection including DFT and molecular docking characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, 33 El Bohouth Str., P.O. 12622, Dokki Giza, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, 33 El Bohouth Str., P.O. 12622, Dokki Giza, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tomatoes are susceptible to microbial spoilage, leading to significant economic losses. This study introduces a novel approach to monitor tomato spoilage. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) films incorporated with sulfur and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (SN–CDs) were developed as pH-sensitive colorimetric sensors for tomatoes. The SN–CDs, derived from red onion peel waste (OPW), exhibited excellent fluorescence properties and antimicrobial activity XPS analysis confirmed the successful synthesis of SN–CDs with incorporated N and S. The incorporation of OPW significantly reduced the Young's modulus of the CMC hydrogel film, likely due to structural disruptions and increased free volume within the film. The color of the prepared colorimetric sensors was changed after tomato spoilage which means the effectiveness of these films in the tomato spoilage detection by naked eye. It exhibited pH-sensitivity because of the presence of flavonoids due to structural changes, including protonation and deprotonation. This pH sensitivity allows for visual indication of pH changes, as demonstrated by the CMC-SN-CDs3 film, which turns yellow in acidic conditions and red in alkaline conditions. The CMC-SN–CDs films displayed a distinct color change in response to pH variations, enabling visual detection of tomato spoilage. DFT calculations and molecular docking studies support the findings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging is a critical element in the food supply chain, safeguarding food products from various threats [1,2]. It acts as a barrier, shielding food from contaminants, microbial growth, and physical damage, thus preserving quality and extending shelf life [3]. Additionally, packaging maintains the sensory qualities of food, such as taste, aroma, and texture, enhancing the consumer experience and boosting brand loyalty [4,5]. It also serves as a communication tool, conveying crucial information like ingredients, nutritional facts, and usage instructions to empower informed consumer choices [6,7]. Lastly, packaging facilitates efficient distribution and logistics, ensuring seamless transportation and minimizing product loss [8,9]. Conventional packaging films, such as plastics, while offering numerous benefits, also present significant limitations. These films often contribute to environmental pollution due to their non-biodegradable nature, leading to long-lasting environmental impact [10]. Additionally, many plastic films lack adequate barrier properties, allowing for the permeation of moisture, oxygen, and other gases, which can accelerate food spoilage and compromise product quality [11]. Furthermore, the production of conventional packaging films often involves energy-intensive processes and the use of harmful chemicals, contributing to carbon emissions and environmental degradation [12–15]. Biodegradable films present a sustainable alternative to conventional plastic packaging. Derived from renewable resources like plant starch, cellulose, and proteins, these films are environmentally friendly and biodegradable under specific conditions [16–18]. They can be engineered to exhibit strong barrier properties, shielding food from moisture, oxygen, and other contaminants [19]. While they may not always fully match the performance of traditional plastics, ongoing research and technological advancements are continually enhancing their capabilities. By adopting biodegradable films, a contribution to a more sustainable and eco-conscious future for food packaging can be made.

Researchers are exploring innovative ways to repurpose carbon dots (CDs) as sensors within biodegradable food packaging. By embedding CD fragments into biodegradable films, these smart packages can monitor food quality parameters [14,20]. The CDs' reflective properties can detect color changes indicating spoilage, while their light absorption and scattering capabilities can measure microbial growth [12,21]. This approach offers sustainability by repurposing waste, enhances food safety through real-time monitoring, and reduces food waste by providing accurate freshness information. Although still in its early stages, this technology holds promise for a more sustainable and safer food packaging future [22]. In a fascinating twist on sustainable technology, researchers are exploring the possibility of using red onion peel waste (OPW) as a key ingredient in the production of CDs [23]. Doping CDs with sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) is a promising strategy to enhance their fluorescence properties, making them ideal candidates for applications in biodegradable cellulose films. By incorporating S and N atoms into the CD structure, their electronic properties can be tuned, leading to increased light absorption and emission [24–27]. Additionally, OPW extract's pH-sensing capabilities stem from the presence of flavonoids [28,29]. These natural pigments are remarkably sensitive to pH changes [29,30]. In acidic solutions, they typically appear yellow. As the pH rises towards neutrality, the color transitions to orange. In alkaline environments, they often display red colors [31,32]. This remarkable color transformation in OPW, driven by certain flavonoids which can exist in different isomeric forms [33]. The relative abundance of these isomers can be influenced by pH, which may subtly affect the overall color of the solution. One major challenge associated with the direct incorporation of sulfur/nitrogen–CDs (SN–CDs) into food packaging materials is their susceptibility to aggregation and sedimentation due to their nanoscale size and high surface energy [34]. This tendency can compromise the uniform dispersion of CDs within the polymer matrix, potentially affecting the desired properties of the packaging material [22]. Additionally, the handling and processing of powdered CDs is complex. To overcome these limitations, alternative strategies, such as the encapsulation of CDs within polymer matrices or the development of stable CD dispersions, may be necessary to ensure their effective and safe integration into food packaging applications [35].

Tomato spoilage is a significant challenge in the food industry, leading to substantial economic losses and food waste [36,37]. Microorganisms such as fungi and bacteria can rapidly colonize tomatoes, causing decay and deterioration [37,38]. Additionally, factors like improper harvesting, storage, and transportation can accelerate spoilage [39,40]. To mitigate these issues, various strategies, including refrigeration, controlled atmosphere storage, and the use of chemical preservatives, are employed [40,41]. However, increasing consumer demand for natural and sustainable food preservation methods has driven the search for alternative approaches, such as the use of edible coatings and natural antimicrobial agents [39,40]. Edible materials, such as polysaccharides (starch, cellulose, alginate, chitosan, gums), proteins (gelatin, casein, whey protein), and lipids (beeswax, shellac), offer sustainable alternatives to traditional plastic packaging [42,43]. These materials can be used to create edible films and coatings that extend shelf life, enhance sensory properties, and reduce food waste. They provide a more environmentally friendly approach to food packaging, minimizing the reliance on non-biodegradable plastics and contributing to a more sustainable future [10].

Sugarcane bagasse (SB) is a promising raw material for the production of cellulose. Its high cellulose content, coupled with its abundance and renewable nature, makes it an attractive alternative to traditional sources of cellulose [44,45]. Cellulose, while an abundant natural polymer, is insoluble in most common solvents, limiting its direct application in film formation [44]. To overcome this challenge, cellulose is often chemically modified to enhance its solubility and processability. One such modification is the conversion of cellulose into carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) [46]. This process involves reacting cellulose with monochloroacetic acid under alkaline conditions. While traditional methods for CMC production can be time-consuming and environmentally unfriendly, microwave-assisted synthesis offers a rapid, energy-efficient, and eco-friendly alternative. By utilizing microwave irradiation, the reaction time can be significantly reduced, and the use of harsh chemicals can be minimized, leading to a more sustainable and efficient production process [12,47]. These materials can be used to create edible films and coatings that extend shelf life, enhance sensory properties, and reduce food waste. To further enhance the functionality of these packaging materials, SN–CDs pH sensors can be incorporated into the films or coatings. These sensors can visually indicate changes in the pH of the food product, providing a clear signal of spoilage. By combining edible materials with pH sensing technology, innovative packaging solutions that are both sustainable and effective in maintaining food quality and safety can be developed.

This study presents a novel, intelligent, naked-eye food packaging film for tomato spoilage detection. The film is composed of a CMC matrix incorporating SN–CDs derived from OPW. These SN–CDs exhibit both pH-sensitive colorimetric and fluorescent properties, along with antimicrobial activity. The film's color changes visually with pH, transitioning from yellow in acidic conditions to red in alkaline environments, allowing for easy detection of tomato spoilage. The incorporation of SN–CDs also enhances the film's antimicrobial properties against common foodborne pathogens like E. coli and S. aureus. DFT calculations and molecular docking studies support the observed interactions and properties of the film. Fluorescence microscopy demonstrates the film's ability to differentiate between E. coli and S. aureus based on differing fluorescence responses. This innovative, sustainable, and cost-effective approach offers a promising solution for monitoring tomato freshness, reducing food waste, and improving food safety.

Red onion peels waste (OPW), sourced from a local Egyptian kitchen, was employed to synthesize SN–CDs. Bagasse agrowaste (SB) was sourced from the Quena Company for Paper Industry, Egypt. Monochloroacetic acid, and urea were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sulfur, nitrogen doped carbon dots (SN–CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize SN–CDs from OPW, a homogeneous mixture of 4 g OPW, 9.33 g NaOH, 9.33 g thiourea, and 100 ml water was prepared to help in solubilizing the cellulosic material inside the OPW with the aid of urea. This mixture was subjected to a series of physical treatments: freezing, thawing, and sonication, followed by microwave irradiation at 700 W for 7 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave assisted preparation of carboxymethyl cellulose from bagasse agrowaste
ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose (15 g) was dissolved in a 30 % NaOH solution containing 18 g monochloroacetic acid by microwave heating. The resulting CMC was precipitated with 70 % methanol, filtered, and dried in an oven [12,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of intelligent naked-eye food packaging hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare CMC-SN–CDs films, 1 g of CMC was dissolved in 50 ml of water at 50 °C. Four different CMC solutions were prepared by mixing the CMC solution with OPW solution in the following volume: 0, 1, 2, and 3 g. Each mixture was stirred continuously for 15 min. The mixtures were then cast into four separate Teflon plates and dried at 60 °C to form films. The resulting films were labeled as CMC hydrogel film, CMC-SN–CDs1, CMC-SN–CDs2, and CMC-SN–CDs3, corresponding to the 0, 1, 2, and 3 g OPW, respectively. Only CMC-SN–CDs3 was employed in this study. The remaining two films were deemed unsuitable due to their low colorimetric response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carboxymethyl cellulose-SN–CDs hydrogel film application to monitor and preserve tomatoes
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh tomato was purchased from the Egyptian local shop of Cairo, Egypt. Two tomatoes were wrapped indirectly with films made of CMC hydrogel film (non-colorimetric film) and CMC-SN–CDs3 (colorimetric film) separately. The tomatoes are in the beakers and the beakers were sealed with the films tightly. Then the tomatoes inside the beakers were subsequently stored in at 25 °C ± 1 for duration of 10 days. Color was examined and pictured by smartphone camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the pH-sensitivity of SN–CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-responsive properties of the SN–CDs extracts were evaluated by adding SN–CDs extract solution (1 g/L) into different buffer solution (pH = 2–14). At pH = 2 (H+ = 0.01 mol/L) and pH = 12 (OH− = 0.01 mol/L). The pH of each solution was adjusted using a calibrated pH meter and appropriate acid or base titrants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the pH-sensitivity of intelligent films
ce:section.ce:para: Following the method described by Boccalon et al. [28], the pH sensitivity of the intelligent film was assessed by immersing it in acidic (pH 3) and basic (pH 12) buffer solutions for 30 s. Color changes were recorded using a smartphone.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM)/energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) images were taken using a Quanta/250-FEG scanning electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). TEM images were acquired using a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope operating at 200 kV. The nanoparticle solution, diluted in ethanol, was deposited onto a carbon-coated copper grid and allowed to dry.
Fluorescence microscopy was performed using a Jasco FP-6500 spectrofluorometer (made in Japan) with a 150-watt xenon arc lamp.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (JASCO V-630, Tokyo, Japan) using a 1 cm path length quartz cell. The quantum yield (QY) was calculated according to the formula:

where “m” is the slope from the plot of fluorescence vs absorbance, the “x” indicates the S, N–CDs, and “st.” refers to methylene blue standard solution in water (0.1 M) [48].

FTIR spectra were recorded using a Mattson 5000 spectrometer (Unicam, United Kingdom) with KBr pellets. The crystallinity index (LOI) and mean hydrogen bond strength (MHBS) were calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3).

where A1425 and A900 refer to the FTIR absorbance at 1425 and 900 cm−1, respectively. In addition, AOH and ACH refer to the FTIR absorbance of the OH and CH peaks, respectively [45,49,50].

The tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB%), and Young's modulus (Y%) of the fabric samples were evaluated using a Lloyd LR 10 k universal testing machine (UK). The testing procedure followed ASTM D-638, with a crosshead speed of 5 mm/min. Prior to testing, samples were cut into rectangular blocks measuring 100 mm (length) × 3 mm (width) × 20 mm (thickness), representing the longitudinal, radial, and tangential directions, respectively. For each sample, an average value was obtained from the results of five individual tensile tests.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted on a Bruker D-8 Advance diffractometer (originating from Germany) to evaluate the crystallinity of the samples. Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å, averaged from Kα 1 and Kα 2) was generated at 40 kV and 40 mA. Each sample was positioned perpendicular to the incident X-ray beam, and continuous scans were acquired across a 2θ range of 5–80 2θ with a step size of 0.053 2θ and a scan rate of 1°/min.

Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 6538) were cultured in agar medium at 37 °C and colony-forming units (CFUs) were calculated. The antibacterial effects of the CMC hydrogel film and CMC-SN–CDs3 were recorded as described by El-Sakhawy et al. [10].

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 W program with the B3LYP hybrid functional and the 6-31G(d) basis set. The Berny optimization technique was employed [51]. Different parameters were investigated via DFT calculations, including some of the optimized geometries and ground state energies, including total energy (ET), the energy of the highest occupied MO EHOMO, the energy of the lowest unoccupied MO ELUMO, the energy gap (Eg), the dipole moment (μ), the absolute hardness (η), the absolute softness (σ), the chemical softness (S), and the additional electronic charge (ΔNmax) [12,21,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the degree of substitution (DS) for CMC
ce:section.ce:para:
To begin the experiment, 37.5 ml of ethanol and 2 g of CMC were combined and stirred. Subsequently, 2.5 ml of nitric acid were added to the mixture. This mixture was then heated to a boil and maintained at this temperature for 2 min. After boiling, the heat source was removed, and the mixture was allowed to cool while continuing to stir for 15 min. The resulting product was then purified by washing it six times with 80 % ethanol heated to 60 °C. The purified product was then dried in an oven at 105 °C for 3 h. To further process the dried product, 1 g of it was dissolved in 100 ml of water. Then, 25 ml of 0.3 Normal sodium hydroxide solution was added to this solution, and the mixture was heated to a boil for 15 min. Finally, the warm solution was titrated with 0.3 Normal hydrochloric acid using phenolphthalein as an indicator. The titration continued until the endpoint was reached, marked by a color change of the indicator [53].
(9)

where;
A = milliequivalent of acid consumed per gm of CMC,
B, C = ml and normality of NaOH,
D, E = ml and normality of HCl,
F = number of gm of CMC used,
162 = gm molecular weight of anhydroglucose unit of cellulose,
and 58 = net increase in molecular weight of anhydroglucose unit for each CMC substituted [10,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS of SN–CDs, fluorescent properties of SN–CDs, and XRD of CMC & SN–CDs
ce:section.ce:para: XPS survey spectrum of SN–CDs (Fig. 1 ) revealed the presence of C, N, O, and S as the dominant elements, with respective atomic percentages of 65.02 %, 4.24 %, 30.74 %, and 0.1 %, confirming the successful incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur into the SN–CDs. High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s was decomposed into three peaks at binding energies of 281.19, 282.37, and 284.83 eV, attributed to the peaks of C–C/C=C, C–S/C–N/C–O, and C=O/C=N respectively [54,55]. Two peaks at 589.87 eV and 532.01 eV in the O 1s spectrum, indicating that the existence of C-O/OH and CO groups [54]. The N 1 s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at binding energies of 396.24 eV, which could be ascribed to the C-N-C/N-H groups. The S 2p spectrum displayed four peaks centered at 159.21, 164.19, 162.92, and 165.37 eV, which were assigned to the R-(O=)S(=O)-R and were associated with the species of -C-SOx- (x = 2, 3, 4) [54,56].

Fig. S1a depicts the fluorescence emission spectra of SN–CDs, excited at 350 nm. These emit at 443 nm, attributed to C=O/C=N/C=S moieties and a peak at 502 nm is ascribed to oxygen vacancy defects [22,57]. The calculated QY for SN–CDs was 37.76 %.

Fig. S1a depicts the fluorescence emission spectra of SN–CDs, excited at 350 nm. These emit at 443 nm, attributed to C=O/C=N/C=S moieties and a peak at 502 nm is ascribed to oxygen vacancy defects [22,57]. The calculated QY for SN–CDs was 37.76 %.

Fig. S1b presents the XRD of CMC and SN–CDs, the CMC exhibiting peaks at 10.35, 20.26, and 22.31°θ. The 20.26, and 22.31°θ peaks prove the amorphous structure of CMC [10]. The SN–CDs exhibiting peaks at 15.88 and 19.58°θ related to the (001) and (002) planes, respectively. This proves the presence of some graphitized structures. The additional peaks appeared at 25.12, 28.47, 32.26, and 35.91° related to the (002), (100), (102), and (103) crystal planes in which (002), (100), and (102) represent graphite (sp2) and (103) represents diamond (sp3) like carbon [48,58].

Fig. S1b presents the XRD of CMC and SN–CDs, the CMC exhibiting peaks at 10.35, 20.26, and 22.31°θ. The 20.26, and 22.31°θ peaks prove the amorphous structure of CMC [10]. The SN–CDs exhibiting peaks at 15.88 and 19.58°θ related to the (001) and (002) planes, respectively. This proves the presence of some graphitized structures. The additional peaks appeared at 25.12, 28.47, 32.26, and 35.91° related to the (002), (100), (102), and (103) crystal planes in which (002), (100), and (102) represent graphite (sp2) and (103) represents diamond (sp3) like carbon [48,58].

The CMC hydrogel film exhibited an initial Young's modulus of 1329 MPa. Upon the incorporation of OPW, this value significantly decreased to 6.52 MPa in the CMC-SN–CDs3 formulation. This decline in mechanical performance within the CMC-SN–CDs3 film can be attributed to several factors. The introduction of OPW likely disrupted the film's compact structure, leading to a reduction in cohesive forces between polymer chains. Furthermore, the high rigidity of the flavonoids within OPW may have hindered the movement of polymer chains, thereby reducing the film's overall flexibility [59,60]. Additionally, the presence of pores, cracks, and an increase in free volume within the film could have diminished its ability to withstand mechanical stress (Fig. 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CMC-SN–CDs hydrogel film formation
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of CMC-SN–CDs hydrogel films involves a complex network of intermolecular interactions between the functional groups of CMC and SN–CDs. These interactions include:

The OH groups from both CMC and SN–CDs can form hydrogen bonds with each other. Additionally, OH groups from CMC can form hydrogen bonds with COOH groups from SN–CDs (Fig. 3 ). Moreover, -SH groups from SN–CDs can react with NH2 groups on neighboring SN–CQDs to form thioamide bonds through a nucleophilic attack mechanism. This reaction results in the elimination of a water molecule [61]. These intermolecular interactions contribute to the cross-linking and network formation within the hydrogel film, leading to its unique properties and applications.

The SN–CDs showed peaks at 3552 cm−1 (NH), 3423 cm−1 (OH), 2954 cm−1 (CH), 2844 cm−1 (SH), 1733 cm−1 (C=O), 1617 cm−1 (amide I), 1440 cm−1 (amide II), 1384 cm−1 (C=C), 1247 cm−1 (O–C=O) and 1139 cm−1 (C–O–C), 989 cm−1 (CO), 884 cm−1 (CN), and 750 cm−1 (CS) [55,62–64]. The presence of NH and CN peaks in the spectra confirms the successful nitrogen doping of the sulfur‑nitrogen doped carbon dots (SN–CDs). Similarly, the CS ad SH peaks indicate the successful sulfur doping of the SN-CDs (Fig. 4 ).

Cellulose showed characteristic bands at 3334 cm−1 (OH), 1627 cm−1 (OH bending of the adsorbed water), 1430 cm−1 (CH2 bending), 1039 cm−1 (C–O–C of pyranose ring), and 894 cm−1 (β-glycosidic linkage between glucose units in cellulose) [10,45,49]. The CMC hydrogel film showed the same cellulosic peaks with an additional peak at 1734 cm−1 (C=O) which prove carboxymethylation reaction [10,65]. The FTIR spectra of CMC-SN–CDs3 showed the same peaks of CMC hydrogel film with absorption peaks at 1544 cm−1 (amide I), 1456 cm−1 (amide II), 918 cm−1 (CN), and 865 cm−1 (CS). In addition, the OH group of CMC hydrogel film was shifted from 3370 cm−1 to 3334 cm−1, which means the strong H-bonding in CMC-SN–CDs3. The calculated MHBS (i.e., 0.85, and 0.86) and LOI (i.e. 1.11 and 1.72) for CMC hydrogel film, and CMC-SN–CDs3, respectively, which means high H-bonding strength between CMC-SN–CDs3 compared to CMC hydrogel film [55,66].

Fig. 5a illustrates the rounded nano-shaped structure of the SN–CDs ranged between 1.68 and 4.56 nm. On the other hand, Fig. 5b-d illustrate the surface morphology of the CMC before film formation, CMC hydrogel film and CMC-SN–CDs3 film. The CMC showed filaments with diameter 21.38–32.29 μm. The CMC hydrogel film showed a film rough surface shaped film with no pore size while the CMC-SN–CDs3 film showed a flowery shape with some pores inside (diameter of pores was ranged between 98.32 and 203.70 nm) due to the presence of SN–CDs. The porosity for CMC-SN–CDs3 was higher than CMC hydrogel film. The high porosity of CMC-SN–CDs3 films facilitates better interaction between the SN–CDs sensor and the external environment, leading to increased sensitivity and faster response times [12].
DFT calculations were employed to study the stability of the CMC, CMC hydrogel film, and CMC-SN–CDs. From Fig. 6 and Table 1 , the results show the following:
(a) The higher μ of CMC-SN–CDs (30.13 Debye) compared to CMC hydrogel film (11.14 Debye) is attributed to the increased electronegativity from S, N, and O atoms in SN–CDs [12,52].
(b) The lower Eg of CMC-SN–CDs (−0.3448 eV) compared to CMC film (0.0419 eV) indicates a strong chemical interaction between CMC and SN–CDs in the composite [21,52].

(C) The lower ET of CMC-SN–CDs3 (−3152.5039 au) compared to CMC hydrogel film (−1134.555 au) indicates a more stable composite structure, making it more difficult to break bonds in the CMC-SN–CDs3 film [21,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity and molecular docking study
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported that the major spoilage tomatoes was caused by Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus [67–70]. The findings revealed that CMC hydrogel film exhibited no antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli or Staphylococcus aureus while CMC-SN–CDs displayed antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus by CFU method equal to 20.00 and 32.15 %, respectively (Fig. 7a). The pH for the hydrogels were 8. The enhanced anti- Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus activity of CMC-SN–CDs3 over CMC hydrogel film is likely attributed to the multifunctional nature of SN-doped CDs. These materials can interact with key bacterial components (lipids, proteins, nucleic acids) via hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interactions [12,22]. Additionally, CDs may induce oxidative stress by generating reactive oxygen species, targeting bacterial surface proteins [71–74].

Fig. 7b&c illustrated the biological activity of CMC-SN–CDs as a ligand against Escherichia coli PDB (2EA9) and Staphylococcus aureus PDB (1KLA) as a receptor. The green lines could represent the formation of a bond or interaction between two molecules (i.e., CMC-SN–CDs and bacteria). The CMC-SN–CDs bound to Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus proteins with bond lengths of ~2.60 and 1.78 Å, respectively. These findings align with experimental results demonstrating the enhanced antimicrobial activity of CMC-SN–CDs compared to CMC hydrogel film. The shorter ligand-protein bond length for Staphylococcus aureus suggests stronger binding and increased protein disruption, leading to enhanced antimicrobial efficacy [12,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carboxymethyl cellulose-SN–CDs hydrogel film as a probe for imaging bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effectiveness of CMC-SN–CDs3 in bacterial detection, Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were selected. Tomatoes, like many other vegetables, are susceptible to contamination by foodborne bacteria, such as Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus [75–79]. Using a fluorescence microscope, red fluorescence from CMC-SN–CDs3 was observed before bacterial exposure (Fig. 8a). Bacterial cells were collected during the exponential growth phase and used for confocal microscopic studies. The cells- CMC-SN–CDs3-conjugates were washed with double distilled water and their images were measured by using a fluorescence microscope. Interestingly, CMC-SN–CDs3 exhibited a different imaging patterns when be contacted with Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. The CMC-SN–CDs3 emitted red fluorescent color. When it is contacted with Escherichia coli it emitted yellow/faint green color while with Staphylococcus aureus it emitted more intense red dots (Fig. 8b, c).

The distinct cell wall structures of Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli influence their interaction with positively charged SN–CDs [22,47]. The Gram-negative bacterium Escherichia coli is shielded by a lipopolysaccharide (LPS) outer membrane [80–82]. This protective layer can hinder the interaction between SN–CDs and the bacterial cell wall [47,83]. LPS molecules may interfere with SN–CD fluorescence by either physically blocking SN–CDs penetration or directly binding to SN–CDs, altering their optical properties, resulting in a different fluorescence response [22,47]. Staphylococcus aureus, with its negatively charged teichoic acid-rich surface, strongly attracts and binds positively charged CDs, leading to more intense red fluorescent spots (Fig. 8c) compared to the CMC-SN–CDs3 before bacterial contact (Fig. 8a). The increased negative charge on the surface of Staphylococcus aureus enhances the electrostatic attraction and binding of positively charged molecules, such as the SN–CDs. These results in a more pronounced red fluorescence compared to the CMC-SN–CDs3 hydrogel film alone [84,85].

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-sensitivity of SN–CDs extracted from OPW and UV study under different pH
ce:section.ce:para: The color of flavonoids, which are present in the OPW as mentioned earlier, is dictated by their interplay with hydrogen ions in the solution. In acidic conditions, they predominantly exhibit yellow hues. As the pH rises, the molecular structure of the flavonoids changes, resulting in a shift towards orange. In alkaline environments, the color further evolves, often displaying red (Fig. 9a). Flavonoids exhibit color changes in response to pH variations. In acidic conditions, flavonoids typically appear yellow due to the protonation of flavonoids, which disrupts the molecule's conjugated system [86]. As it is mentioned before that OPW contain flavonoids. As the pH rises to neutral, partial deprotonation occurs, increasing conjugation and causing a shift to an orange color. Finally, in alkaline environments, flavonoids can undergo more significant structural changes, such as complete deprotonation and possible tautomerization or formation of quinones and/or phenolates, leading to a red hue [87]. These color changes reflect the dynamic relationship between flavonoid structure, electron delocalization, and light absorption across the pH spectrum (Fig. 9b). In the context of the structure change under pH variation, the general principle of carbonyl oxygen protonation remains valid. Flavonoids, typically possess at least one carbonyl group (C=O). While protonation usually occurs on the carbonyl oxygen, forming an oxonium ion stabilized by resonance within the flavonoid structure, carbon protonation is less likely due to carbon's lower electronegativity and the potential disruption of aromaticity. However, factors like the presence of electron-donating or -withdrawing substituents on the aromatic rings and the specific arrangement of rings within the flavonoid skeleton can influence the reactivity. Importantly, in alkaline media, the carbonyl group of flavonoids can undergo deprotonation (loss of a proton) to form a resonance-stabilized enolate ion. This deprotonation is facilitated by the presence of a strong base in the alkaline environment. The formation of the enolate ion significantly impacts the reactivity of the flavonoid, influencing its potential for further reactions such as nucleophilic additions or condensations. The ether group inside the ring of flavonoids can make the same behavior [86]. To evaluate pH sensitivity, a test was conducted on the CMC-SN–CDs3 film, which exhibited a yellow color under acidic conditions and a red color under alkaline conditions (Fig. 9c). This demonstrates that the pH-sensitive CMC-SN–CDs3 film can visually indicate pH changes without requiring specialized equipment. The distinct color change is easily observable with the naked eye, providing a straightforward and convenient approach for monitoring pH levels. Fig. 9d presents the UV–vis spectrum of SN–CDs under different pH, at pH 8 it exhibiting typical UV region absorption with an intense peak at 264.5 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds and a shoulder peak at 356.5 nm assigned to the n-π* transition of CO bonds [21,88]. After protonation, a shift was occurred to 274.5 and 351.0 at pH 2 which meaning protonation to oxoium. Protonation of specific sites within the flavonoid structure, such as hydroxyl groups on the aromatic rings, can extend the conjugated system, leading to increased electron delocalization. This extended conjugation lowers the energy gap between electronic states within the molecule, allowing it to absorb lower energy photons (longer wavelengths) for electronic transitions, resulting in a red shift of the UV absorption peak (Fig. 9d). This is often referred to as a red shift or bathochromic shift. In the alkaline medium, for example pH 14, the shift was occurred to 516.0 which meaning the phenolic hydroxyl groups of flavonoids deprotonate, forming phenolate ions (i.e. enolate formation). This increases electron density and extends the conjugated system, leading to a red shift. The disappearance of the CC peak in the UV–Vis spectrum of flavonoids in alkaline media can be attributed to significant alterations in the electronic structure arising from the formation of phenolate ions upon deprotonation of phenolic hydroxyl groups. This deprotonation disrupts the conjugation system associated with the CC bond, leading to changes in electronic transitions and potentially diminishing or eliminating the absorbance observed for this bond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carboxymethyl cellulose-SN–CDs hydrogel film as pH-sensor for tomatoes spoilage by naked eye
ce:section.ce:para: Perfectly ripe tomatoes spoil within 2–3 days when left out of the refrigerator [89,90]. The pH of tomatoes during spoilage is 5.2 [91]. This pH level is crucial for maintaining the tomato's quality, texture, and flavor [92–95]. This pH range can be attributed to the breakdown of the tomato's cellular structure and the activity of microorganisms [94–96]. Notably, the spoilage of tomatoes directly influences the color response of the CMC-SN–CDs3 film (Fig. 9e), resulting in a significant color change. In contrast to the pure CMC hydrogel film, the developed CMC-SN–CDs3 film maintains its structural integrity, which can be attributed to the antimicrobial properties of SN–CDs incorporated within the CMC-SN–CDs3 film. This demonstrates the potential of CMC-SN–CDs3 for real-time monitoring of tomato spoilage. The observed color transition of the CMC-SN–CDs3 film from red to light red over time reflects its sensitivity to the spoilage process of the tomatoes it covers. Conversely, the pure CMC hydrogel film was degraded after tomato spoilage, undergoing hydrolysis and structural damage due to bacterial activity while the CMC-SN–CDs3 film didn't degrade.

The SN–CDs, derived from OPW, were synthesized, and their incorporation into the CMC matrix was performed to enhance the film's properties. XPS analysis confirmed the successful synthesis of SN–CDs with incorporated nitrogen and sulfur. The CMC hydrogel film, incorporating OPW, exhibited a significant reduction in Young's modulus, likely attributed to structural disruptions and increased free volume. Notably, the presence of flavonoids imparted pH sensitivity to the film, resulting in a visually discernible color change from yellow in acidic conditions to red in alkaline environments due to pH-dependent structural changes within the flavonoids. The study of flavonoids which present within OPW reveals their remarkable ability to act as natural pH indicators. Their color variations across the pH spectrum, ranging from yellow in acidic conditions to orange in neutral and red in alkaline environments, stem from the intricate interplay between their molecular structure, protonation-deprotonation processes, and electron delocalization. This pH-dependent color change has practical implications, enabling visual monitoring of pH levels without the need for specialized equipment. The CMC-SN–CDs3 film exemplifies this capability, showcasing a distinct color change from yellow to red as pH shifts from acidic to alkaline. This straightforward approach underscores the potential of flavonoids as accessible and user-friendly tools for pH assessment. For that, the resulting CMC-SN–CDs hydrogel film which contain flavonoids exhibited excellent pH sensitivity, enabling visual detection of food spoilage. The film also demonstrated significant antimicrobial activity against common foodborne pathogens, such as Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. This dual functionality of pH sensing and antimicrobial activity makes the CMC-SN–CDs hydrogel film a promising candidate for tomatoes packaging.
